1: Isaiah 5 opens with the Song of the Vineyard (Isaiah 5:1-7), the most developed parable in the prophetic literature and the prototype for Jesus's parable of the wicked tenants (Matthew 21:33-44; Mark 12:1-12; Luke 20:9-19). The prophet presents himself as a minstrel singing a love song (shir dodi, song of my beloved) about his beloved's vineyard. The vineyard owner is YHWH, the vineyard is Israel. The song begins seductively in the wedding-song genre before revealing itself as a lawsuit. 'My beloved had a vineyard on a rich and fertile hillside' — keren ben-shamen, literally 'on a horn of a son of oil,' = a hillside of exceptional fertility (olive-growing conditions).
2: 'He dug it up and cleared away its stones, and planted it with the finest vines; he built a watchtower inside it and cut out a winepress in it. He expected it to produce fine grapes, but it produced only wild, sour grapes' — the vineyard owner performed every preparation a skilled vintner would make: clearing stones (an arduous, multi-year task on Judean hillsides), planting sorek (the finest Israelite grape variety, also in Genesis 49:11 and Jeremiah 2:21), building the watchtower (migdal), cutting the winepress (yeqev). Every element represents YHWH's care for Israel (the Exodus, Sinai, the land, the temple, the Davidic covenant). Yet the outcome was wild/sour grapes (be'ushim, wild/worthless grapes).
3: 'And now, you people of Jerusalem and you citizens of Judah, judge between me and my vineyard' — the shift from third-person narrative to direct address catches the audience off guard. The listeners are asked to render a legal verdict (shifte-na, please judge) between YHWH and his vineyard. By the time they render the verdict in Isaiah 5:4 (implying the vineyard deserves punishment), they have condemned themselves — the reversal is complete when Isaiah 5:7 reveals the vineyard = Judah. The rhetorical trap of self-condemnation through narrative is also deployed by Nathan in his parable to David (2 Samuel 12:1-7) and Jesus in his parable of the tenants.
4: 'What more could have been done for my vineyard that I did not do? Why then, when I expected good grapes, did it produce only wild grapes?' — the divine bewilderment at covenant failure. The question is not rhetorical evasion but genuine incomprehension: YHWH provided everything (covenant, law, land, temple, prophets, deliverance from enemies) and received rebellious ingratitude. The contrast between divine expectation (waited/hoped, vayqav) and actual outcome (sour grapes) is the central tragedy. The same bewilderment appears in Jeremiah 2:5: 'What did your ancestors find wrong in me that they strayed from me?'
5: 'Now let me tell you what I am going to do to my vineyard: I will take away its protective hedge, and it will be consumed; I will break down its wall, and it will be trampled underfoot' — the judgment declared by the vineyard owner: removing the protective hedge (mesukato, its thorn hedge) and wall (gederato, its stone wall) that kept out animals and raiders. In ancient Israelite agriculture, a vineyard's hedge/wall was its primary protection; their removal leaves it defenseless. This is YHWH's withdrawal of covenant protection from Israel — the removal of the blessing-shield that sustained the nation. The covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:25 ('The LORD will cause you to be defeated before your enemies') is the content of this removal.
6: 'I will make it a ruin: it will not be pruned or cultivated, and briers and thorns will overrun it. I will command the clouds to withhold their rain from it' — the specific abandonment: no pruning (yizzamer, regular vine-pruning necessary for fruitful growth) and no cultivation (ye'ader, hoeing the soil around the vines). Left untended, the vineyard reverts to thorns and thistles — the curse of Genesis 3:18 applied to the whole land. Most significantly, YHWH commands the clouds not to rain (al-hametal metar) — direct divine intervention in the rainfall cycle. In Canaanite theology, Baal provided rain; YHWH here demonstrates that he controls the rain Baal supposedly supplied, and he can withhold it.
7: 'For the vineyard of the LORD of hosts is the house of Israel, and the men of Judah are his cherished planting. He looked for justice, but saw only oppression; for righteousness, but heard only cries of distress' — the interpretation and the famous Hebrew paronomasia: waited for mishpat (justice) but found mispach (bloodshed); for tsedaqah (righteousness) but heard tse'aqah (crying out). The wordplay is untranslatable: the sounds are almost identical but the meanings are inverted. The parable's beauty becomes a weapon — the vinegrower who invested everything in his vineyard received the precise inverse of what he sought. The 'cries of distress' (tse'aqah) echoes the cry of Sodom (Genesis 18:20-21) and the cry of the oppressed slaves in Egypt (Exodus 3:7).
8: 'Woe to those who accumulate house after house and absorb field after field until no space remains' — the first of six woe-oracles (Isaiah 5:8, 11, 18, 20, 21, 22). This woe targets latifundialization: the consolidation of small landholdings into large estates owned by the wealthy elite. The Jubilee law (Leviticus 25:23-28) was designed precisely to prevent this: every 50 years, land reverted to its original tribal allotment to ensure distributive justice. The woe condemns those who bought out or seized the small farmers' plots. Micah 2:2 is a parallel: 'They covet fields and seize them, and houses, and take them; they oppress a man and his household.' Amos 5:11 addresses the same class.
9: 'The LORD of hosts has sworn in my hearing: Many houses, large and fine as they are, will stand empty and desolate' — the judicial curse matching the crime: those who accumulated houses will see them empty. The punishment follows the pattern of Deuteronomy 28:30 (the covenant curse of building a house but not living in it, planting a vineyard but not enjoying its fruit). The oath formula ('sworn in my hearing') marks this as irrevocable: YHWH's sworn word cannot be recalled. The specific punishment is desolation (shamamah, desert/emptiness) — the Latin desolatio rendered the same Hebrew word used in the 'Abomination of Desolation' passages of Daniel.
10: 'Ten acres of vineyard will produce only a single bath of wine, and a homer of seed will yield only an ephah of grain' — the specific agricultural curse. Ten acres (ten tsamidim, ten yokes — the amount one yoke of oxen could plow in a day) producing only one bath of wine (approximately 22 liters — a 10:1 ratio below normal yield). A homer of seed (a donkey-load, approximately 220 liters) yielding only an ephah (22 liters) = a 10:1 loss on seed investment. Both figures represent catastrophic agricultural failure — a 90% shortfall. This is precisely the curse of Leviticus 26:20 ('your land will not yield its produce') made precise and quantitative.
11: 'Woe to those who are up at dawn chasing strong drink, who linger into the night with wine burning in their veins!' — the second woe targets the aristocratic drinking culture: rising at dawn to begin drinking (shachar, the dawn) and extending into the night. The shechar (strong drink) and yayin (wine) are the standard pair of intoxicants in the OT. The social context: the wealthy elite who feast continuously (Isaiah 5:12) while the poor are ground down (Isaiah 3:15). Amos 6:4-6 describes the same Israelite elite 'who drink wine by the bowlful and use the finest lotions' while unconcerned with the ruin of Joseph. Ecclesiastes 10:16-17 contrasts the land cursed by feasting rulers with the land blessed by rulers who eat at the proper time.
12: 'At their feasts there are lyre and harp, tambourine and flute and plenty of wine — but they give no thought to what the LORD has done, and they do not consider the work of his hands' — the five instruments of the feast (kinnor vavel, tof vechalil vayayin) are the full orchestra of Israelite celebration. The instruments themselves are not wrong — the same instruments appear in temple worship (Psalms 150:3-5). The problem is the cognitive/theological failure: they do not consider (hibbit, look attentively at) the work of YHWH's hands (po'al yadav, what his hands have done). The feast culture produces willful amnesia about YHWH's covenant acts. Amos 6:5 ('you pluck away on your harps like David and improvise on musical instruments') similarly indicts musical self-indulgence disconnected from covenant loyalty.
13: 'Therefore my people will go into exile for want of understanding; their distinguished men will be starving and their masses dying of thirst' — the logical consequence: exile (galah) because of lack of knowledge (beli-da'at, without knowing). The da'at (knowledge/knowing) is the covenantal knowledge of YHWH that Israel should have had (Hosea 4:6: 'my people are destroyed from lack of knowledge'). The ironic inversion: the distinguished men (kavod, honorable ones) will be famished, and the masses (hamon, the multitude) will be parched with thirst — the same thirst they tried to cure with wine (Isaiah 5:11) now becomes literal judgment.
14: 'Therefore Sheol has expanded its appetite and opened its mouth without limit; Jerusalem's splendour and her crowds and her revelry and all who celebrate in her will descend into it' — Sheol personified as a voracious beast opening its gullet without limit. The verb hirchiva nefesh (has widened its throat/appetite) uses the same nefesh (throat/appetite/soul) that is expanded to receive Jerusalem's population. In ancient Israelite cosmology, Sheol was the realm of the dead below the earth — not hell in the Christian sense but the universal abode of the departed (Job 3:17-19; Psalms 6:5). The specific list of Jerusalem's inhabitants going down: its kavod (glory), its hamon (multitude), its sha'on (tumult), and the exulting ones (alizah) — all the expressions of social vitality swallowed.
15: 'Human beings are bowed down and each person humbled, and the eyes of the proud are cast down' — the Day-of-the-LORD theme from Isaiah 2 (Isaiah 2:9, 11, 17) restated: the universal humbling of human pride. This verse deliberately echoes Isaiah 2:9 ('Mankind is brought low and every person humbled') indicating that the six woes of Isaiah 5 are the specified content of the general divine judgment announced in Isaiah 2. The proud gaze (einei gevohim, haughty eyes — the same physiognomy of pride as Isaiah 2:11) is cast down. The 'eyes cast down' reverses the posture of the confident pride of the feast-goers of Isaiah 5:12.
16: 'But the LORD of hosts is exalted in justice, and the Holy God proves himself holy through righteousness' — the theological center of the chapter: YHWH's exaltation (yisgav, is raised up) comes through judgment (mishpat). The Holy God (ha'el haqqadosh) is sanctified/shows his holiness (veyiqqadesh) through righteousness (bitsedaqah). The divine holiness (qedushah) and righteousness (tsedaqah) are not separate attributes but mutually defining: YHWH's holiness is not an abstract metaphysical purity but an active moral righteousness that executes justice. The same connection appears in the trisagion of 6:3 and is central to Isaiah's theology of the Holy One of Israel.
17: 'Then lambs will graze as in their own meadow, and wandering flocks will feed among the ruins of the wealthy' — the reversal: where the wealthy had their estates (the accumulated houses and fields of Isaiah 5:8), sheep and goats will graze. The abandoned ruins of the latifundia aristocracy become pastureland for those who wander (gerim, sojourners/wanderers — typically the most marginal social category). The inversion is the judgment matching the crime: those who absorbed field after field receive a land that reverts to pasture. Isaiah 32:14 develops the same image of cities becoming open pasture after judgment.
18: 'Woe to those who drag along iniquity with cords of deception and haul sin behind them like a cart on thick ropes' — the third woe targets deliberate, willful sin-accumulation. The imagery of hauling iniquity (avon) with cords of falsehood (chavle hashav) and sin (chata'ah) with cart ropes (ka'avot ha'agalah, like the ropes of a wagon) suggests a person who has built an entire industrial apparatus for sinning — sin is not accidental but a heavy load they transport by design. The cords of falsehood/deception (shav, vanity/falsehood) suggest that the accumulation of sin is sustained by self-deception: they have deceived themselves into thinking the sin-wagon can be managed.
19: 'Who say: Let God hurry up, let him get on with his work so we can see it; let the purpose of the Holy One of Israel come close and happen so we can find out what it is!' — the sarcastic challenge to YHWH to prove himself: if you're going to judge us, get on with it! The phrase 'purpose of the Holy One of Israel' (atat qedosh Yisrael) uses Isaiah's distinctive divine epithet in a mocking context. The mockers are functionally atheistic not in believing YHWH doesn't exist but in treating him as powerless to act. The challenge to YHWH to perform on demand is similar to the taunt of Psalms 22:8 ('let the LORD rescue him, since he delights in him') applied to Jesus on the cross.
20: 'Woe to those who call evil good and good evil, who substitute darkness for light and light for darkness, who swap bitter for sweet and sweet for bitter!' — the fourth woe: the moral-epistemic inversion of ethical categories. The triple chiastic reversal (evil/good, darkness/light, bitter/sweet) describes a complete inversion of the moral order — not merely doing wrong while knowing it is wrong, but reconceptualizing evil as good, darkness as light. This is the deepest form of moral corruption: not a failure of will but a failure of moral perception. Paul's description of the pagan world in Romans 1:18-32 traces the progression from suppressing truth to exchanging natural for unnatural to becoming 'inventors of evil.' The Isaiah woe names what Paul describes as its endpoint.
21: 'Woe to those who consider themselves wise and regard themselves as shrewd!' — the fifth woe: autonomous wisdom, wisdom 'in their own eyes' (chachamim be'einehem, wise in their own eyes). Proverbs 3:7 warns against this exact posture: 'Do not be wise in your own eyes; fear the LORD and shun evil.' The wise person in Proverbs is one whose wisdom is derived from and accountable to YHWH's instruction (Proverbs 1:7: 'the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom'). The self-validated sage who requires no external correction from YHWH's revelation is the fool who appears wise to himself (Proverbs 26:12: 'Do you see a man wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.').
22: 'Woe to those who are heroes when it comes to drinking wine, champions at blending strong liquor' — the sixth woe returns to the drunkenness theme of Isaiah 5:11-12 but now with satirical heroism language. The gibborim (mighty men/warriors — the elite military class) and anshei chayil (men of valor) are the terms for Israel's warrior heroes. Here they are champions at drinking — their 'heroism' is measured in consumption capacity and skill at mixing drinks (mezeg shechar, blending strong drink). The satirical use of military honor vocabulary for drinking prowess deflates the warrior class by revealing their actual vocation.
23: 'Who let the guilty off the hook for a bribe and strip the righteous of their legal due!' — the judicial corruption connected to the drinking culture: the drinking heroes (Isaiah 5:22) are the same leaders who corrupt the courts. 'Let the guilty off the hook' (matsdiqe rasha, who justify/acquit the wicked) and 'strip the righteous' (tsedaqat tsadiqim yisiru mehem, who remove the righteousness from the righteous) — the complete inversion of the judicial function. Proverbs 17:15: 'Acquitting the guilty and condemning the innocent — the LORD detests them both.' The bribe (shochad) given as payment for the corrupt verdict is the mechanism of legal inversion, the same corruption as Isaiah 1:23.
24: 'Therefore, as fire consumes stubble and dry grass collapses in flame, so their root will rot and their blossom scatter like dust; for they have rejected the instruction of the LORD of hosts and despised the word of the Holy One of Israel' — the cumulative judicial verdict on the six woe-groups. The fire-consumes-stubble imagery echoes Isaiah 1:31 (the powerful becoming tinder). The specific botanical metaphor: root (shoresh) rotting and blossom/flower (perach) dispersing like dust — the entire agricultural plant, from root to flower, collapses. The root cause is stated: rejection of the torah of YHWH of hosts and contempt for the word of the Holy One of Israel — the covenant instruction that would have produced justice (Isaiah 5:7) is the very thing they despised.
25: 'For this reason the LORD's anger burned against his people; he stretched out his hand and struck them, and the mountains trembled; their bodies lay like rubbish in the middle of the streets. Despite all this, his anger has not turned away — his hand is still outstretched' — the ominous refrain 'his anger has not turned away and his hand is still outstretched' (lo-shav apo ve'od yado netuyah) appears four more times in Isaiah (Isaiah 9:12, 17, 21; Isaiah 10:4) as the closing line of a cascading series of judgments. The stretched-out hand is the classic gesture of divine judgment (Exodus 7:5: 'I will stretch out my hand against Egypt'). The mountains trembling and bodies strewn in the streets reflects the Assyrian campaigns' actual devastation of the Judean countryside.
26: 'He raises a signal for a distant nation and whistles for it from the far ends of the earth, and look — it comes at full speed!' — YHWH as the military commander who deploys Assyria as his instrument of judgment. The 'signal' (nes, standard/flag) raised on a mountain and the 'whistle' (sharaq, hissing signal) for the distant nation (goy merachok, nation from afar) are the military deployment commands. The image of YHWH whistling for Assyria as a beekeeper whistles for bees (Isaiah 7:18: 'the LORD will whistle for the bee from the land of Assyria') reveals that the terrifying Assyrian army is YHWH's instrument, not an autonomous power. The swift arrival ('it comes at full speed!') immediately begins the terrifying description of Isaiah 5:27-30.
27: 'Not one of them is tired, not one trips or falls; not one drowses or sleeps; not a belt is slack and not a sandal-strap is snapped' — the description of the Assyrian army in perfect military readiness. The negatives (no fatigue, no stumbling, no drowsiness, no loose equipment) paint a picture of a machine-like military force in peak condition. The sandal-strap not snapped is a detail of parade-ground perfection: even the soldiers' footwear is in order. Ancient military campaigning over hundreds of miles inevitably produced the fatigue and equipment failures that Isaiah's description denies. This is the supernatural vigor of YHWH's instrument of judgment — a force that does not degrade during its long march from Assyria.
28: 'Their arrows are sharp and every bow is strung taut; their warhorses' hooves strike like flint, and their chariot wheels spin like a tornado' — the Assyrian military technology described in fearful detail: sharp arrows, taut bows, the iron-shod hooves (flint-sparking on stone roads), and the chariot wheels like a whirlwind (saphah kesufah, a storm/whirlwind). The Assyrian army was the most advanced military force of the 8th-7th century BCE, with standing armies, iron weapons, battering rams, and systematic logistics. Assyrian annals and palace reliefs confirm the technologies Isaiah describes — archers, cavalry, war chariots. Isaiah's audience would have recognized each detail with terror.
29: 'Their battle cry is like the roar of a lion — like the roar of young lions they snarl, seize their prey, and carry it off with no one to deliver it' — the lion imagery for military power: the roar of the mature lion (layish, the old lion) and the growling of young lions (kefir). The lion seizing prey and carrying it off 'with no one to deliver it' (maphlit, no deliverer/rescuer) completes the terror picture: the prey has no advocate, no rescuer. In the ANE, the lion was the symbol of royal power and military dominance (Assyrian palace reliefs show lion hunts as royal sport, asserting the king's mastery over nature's most powerful predator). Using lion imagery for Assyria appropriates this royal symbolism for YHWH's chosen instrument.
30: 'In that day they will roar over it like the roaring of the sea. And if someone looks out over the land — only darkness and anguish; the light is blotted out by heavy cloud' — the closing image: the noise of the approaching army like the sea's roar (qol hayyam, the voice of the sea — associated with chaos and divine power; Psalms 93:3-4). Looking at the land: only darkness (choshek, dark) and anguish (tsarah, distress), the light darkened by cloud (va'or chashach ba'arafileyha, the light is dark in its clouds). The triple dark vocabulary (choshek, tsarah, chashach) closes the vineyard song's judgment with total atmospheric darkness — the opposite of the light promised to the righteous. This darkness sets up the light promised in Isaiah 9:2.
